label_inferred: Crude Oil Tanker Shipping — Freight Rates
demand_drivers_inferred: Strait of Hormuz closure / Middle East conflict rerouting VLCC traffic to longer Cape routes
VLCC capacity tightening: longer routes consume more vessel-days per cargo
Sanctions-driven dark fleet shadow tankers absorbing capacity outside regulated markets
Oil supply disruption forcing non-Middle East producers to increase seaborne volumes
differentiation_inferred: Tanker shipping is a freight services business, not an oil producer or commodity holder. P&L is driven by daily charter rates (VLCC spot and time charter) and route distance, not oil price direction. A rising oil price can coincide with falling tanker rates (if supply is disrupted at source, fewer barrels move). Conversely, a Strait of Hormuz closure raises tanker rates even if oil price falls due to demand destruction. Entirely different factor exposure from oil_majors_integrated, oil_services_equipment, or lng_natural_gas. BWET holds VLCC freight futures — no commodity price exposure at all.

closest_existing_sectors.similarity_reason: Both in the oil supply chain.
closest_existing_sectors.why_insufficient: Oil majors own the cargo and benefit from oil price increases. Tanker operators earn freight on the transport leg regardless of oil price direction. Their incentives can be opposite: oil supply disruption hurts majors (less volume), helps tanker operators (longer routes per barrel moved).

Infrastructure_core covers regulated toll roads, airports, ports with inflation-linked contracts and stable cash flows. Tanker shipping is unregulated, highly cyclical, driven by spot rates that can swing 10x in a single quarter. Mean-reverting by nature. Opposite risk profile.

proxies.note: Breakwave Dry Bulk Shipping ETF — same manager as BWET but dry bulk (iron ore, coal, grain), not crude oil. Different cycle driver.

evidence.description: BWET (Breakwave Tanker Shipping ETF) up +1,406% year-over-year as of 2026-06-01. Strait of Hormuz closure on 2026-02-28 following US-Israel-Iran conflict. VLCC freight rates spiked as Middle East crude was rerouted around Cape of Good Hope. BWET holds VLCC freight futures (90% VLCC, 10% Suezmax). $100K invested at start of 2026 = $764K by April 2026. Short interest surged 142% in February 2026 indicating significant reversion risk.
evidence.source_query: tanker shipping ETF BWET Strait of Hormuz 2026

rejection_reason: Geopolitical event catalyst, no sector estructural. El movimiento de BWET (+1.406%) está 100% explicado por el cierre del Estrecho de Ormuz (2026-02-28) — evento discreto y reversible. Freight rates son mean-reverting por naturaleza. Sin el evento, no hay tesis. Registrado como CatalystEvent cat_20260228_hormuz_closure con is_priced_in_estimate: 1.0.
notes: STRUCTURAL CAUTION: BWET is driven by a single geopolitical event (Strait of Hormuz closure). Freight rates are mean-reverting by nature. BWET has 3.50% expense ratio and holds near-dated freight futures with structural roll cost. This is a high-conviction TACTICAL play, not a permanent structural sector. The question for promotion is whether tanker shipping deserves a permanent taxonomy entry or should be treated as a geopolitical event catalyst under existing sectors (oil_majors_integrated + geopolitical type). Consider: does the theme persist if Hormuz reopens? If no, this may be better handled as a CatalystEvent rather than a new sector.
